name: Kubernetes
description: An open-source container orchestration platform for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. The industry standard for production container orchestration.
overview: Kubernetes (K8s) is the leading open-source platform for managing containerized workloads and services. Originating from Google's internal Borg system (15+ years of production experience), Kubernetes was open-sourced in 2014 and has become the standard API for cloud-native computing. It automates container deployment, scaling, load balancing, service discovery, rolling updates, self-healing, and resource management across clusters. Kubernetes abstracts underlying infrastructure, enabling consistent deployments across on-premise, cloud, and hybrid environments. Its extensible architecture allows integration with service meshes (Istio), monitoring (Prometheus), CI/CD (ArgoCD), and serverless (Knative). While powerful, Kubernetes has a steep learning curve due to its complex architecture, extensive configuration options, and new paradigms (declarative vs imperative, desired state vs current state). Mastery of Kubernetes is essential for modern platform engineering and cloud-native development.

learningPath.title: Kubernetes Fundamentals & Architecture
learningPath.description: Understand Kubernetes architecture, core components, cluster setup, and basic kubectl commands.
topics: What is Kubernetes? Why orchestration? Problems it solves
Kubernetes architecture: control plane vs worker nodes
Control plane components: API Server, etcd, Scheduler, Controller Manager
Node components: kubelet, kube-proxy, container runtime (containerd, CRI-O)
Understanding pods: the smallest deployable unit
kubectl: the Kubernetes CLI — commands, context, config
Setting up local clusters: Minikube, kind, k3s, Docker Desktop K8s
Kubernetes API and declarative configuration (YAML manifests)
Namespaces: multi-tenancy and isolation
kubectl apply, get, describe, logs, exec, delete
kubectl port-forward for local access
Contexts and kubeconfig files
Kubernetes dashboard and Web UIs (Octant, Lens, OpenLens)
Understanding desired state vs current state reconciliation
projects: Install Minikube and deploy nginx pod

learningPath.title: Pods, Workloads & Controllers

Pod states: Pending, Running, Succeeded, Failed, CrashLoopBackOff
Pod probes: liveness, readiness, startup — HTTP, TCP, command
Resource requests and limits (CPU, memory) — QoS classes
Deployments: declarative updates, replica management, rollout strategies
Rolling updates: maxSurge, maxUnavailable, minReadySeconds
Rollbacks: undo, rollout history, revision limits
ReplicaSet: how deployments manage replicas
StatefulSets: stable network IDs, persistent storage, ordered deployment
StatefulSet: headless services, pod identity, scaling, rolling updates
DaemonSets: running pods on every (or selected) node
Jobs: running pods to completion — parallelism, backoff, completions
CronJobs: scheduled job execution — cron syntax, concurrency policy
HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA): auto-scaling based on CPU/memory/custom metrics
VerticalPodAutoscaler (VPA): auto-adjusting resource requests
PodDisruptionBudget: maintaining availability during disruptions
projects: Deploy web app with rolling update strategy

Service discovery with DNS (CoreDNS): <service>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local

Ingress: path-based routing, host-based routing, TLS termination
Gateway API: the evolution of Ingress (GatewayClass, Gateway, HTTPRoute)
Headless services for StatefulSet pod DNS
Network Policies: pod-level firewalls, ingress/egress rules
kube-proxy: iptables, IPVS, userspace modes
DNS for pods and services: pod DNS, /etc/resolv.conf
External DNS: integrating Kubernetes DNS with cloud DNS
Multi-cluster networking (Cilium ClusterMesh, Submariner)
Service meshes: Istio architecture, mTLS, traffic management

learningPath.title: Storage & ConfigMaps
learningPath.description: Manage configuration, secrets, and persistent storage in Kubernetes applications.

Secrets: creating, encoding, consuming (env vars, volumes, image pull secrets)

Persistent Volumes (PV): static provisioning, storage classes, reclaim policies
Persistent Volume Claims (PVC): requesting storage, access modes
Storage Classes: dynamic provisioning — cloud (EBS, EFS, GCEPersistentDisk, AzureDisk)
CSI (Container Storage Interface) drivers

Volume snapshots and cloning

learningPath.title: Security & RBAC
learningPath.description: Secure Kubernetes clusters: authentication, authorization, RBAC, Pod Security, and admission controllers.
topics: Kubernetes security model: authentication, authorization, admission control
Service Accounts: creating, using, mounting API credentials
RBAC: Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings
RBAC best practices: least privilege, principle of minimum access
Pod Security Standards: privileged, baseline, restricted
Pod Security Admission (PSA): built-in admission controller (K8s 1.23+)
Pod Security Policies (PSP) — deprecated, migration to PSA
Security Context: runAsUser, runAsGroup, fsGroup, capabilities, seLinuxOptions, seccomp
Network Policies: pod-level network segmentation
Admission Controllers: built-in (NamespaceLifecycle, LimitRanger, PodSecurity) and custom
MutatingAdmissionWebhook and ValidatingAdmissionWebhook
Open Policy Agent (OPA) / Gatekeeper: policy as code for K8s
Kyverno: Kubernetes-native policy management
Container image security: image scanning, trusted registries, ImagePolicyWebhook
kube-bench for CIS benchmarks
Audit logging: enabling, configuring, collecting
API server security: TLS, authentication webhooks, certificates

interviewQuestions.question: What is a Pod and why is it the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes?
interviewQuestions.answer: A Pod is the smallest deployable unit that represents a single instance of a running process in Kubernetes. It wraps one or more containers with shared storage/network (same IP, port space), and a specification for how to run them (resources, restart policy, probes). Pods enable: co-located helper containers (sidecars), shared volumes between containers, and unified lifecycle management. Containers in a pod are always scheduled on the same node.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between a Deployment and a StatefulSet?
interviewQuestions.answer: Deployment: for stateless applications — pods are interchangeable, can be created/destroyed in any order, without persistent identity. StatefulSet: for stateful applications — each pod gets a unique, stable identity (ordinal index), ordered creation/scaling/deletion, persistent storage per pod via volumeClaimTemplates. Use Deployment for stateless apps (web servers, APIs). Use StatefulSet for databases, message queues, and any app needing stable network identity.

interviewQuestions.question: How does Kubernetes service discovery work?
interviewQuestions.answer: Kubernetes provides DNS-based service discovery via CoreDNS. Each Service gets a DNS name: <service>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local. Pods resolve service names to ClusterIPs. For headless services (clusterIP: None), DNS returns pod IPs directly. kube-proxy implements virtual IPs (iptables/IPVS) and load balancing. Service environment variables (SERVICE_HOST, SERVICE_PORT) are injected but DNS is preferred.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the Kubernetes controller pattern.
interviewQuestions.answer: Controllers are control loops that watch the state of resources via the API server and make changes to drive the current state toward the desired state (reconciliation loop). Examples: ReplicaSet controller ensures correct number of pod replicas, Deployment controller manages ReplicaSet rollouts, EndpointSlice controller updates endpoints when pods change. Custom controllers can be built for custom resources (operator pattern).

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between a Service and an Ingress?
interviewQuestions.answer: Service: internal load balancer providing stable network endpoint for pods, operates at L4 (TCP/UDP), types: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer. Ingress: external HTTP/HTTPS routing to services, operates at L7, provides host/path-based routing, TLS termination, and name-based virtual hosting. Ingress requires an Ingress Controller (NGINX, Traefik, HAProxy) to be installed in the cluster.

interviewQuestions.question: How does Kubernetes handle persistent storage?
interviewQuestions.answer: Storage is managed through PV (PersistentVolume) and PVC (PersistentVolumeClaim). PV is cluster storage resource provisioned by admin or dynamically via StorageClass. PVC is a request for storage by a user — binds to matching PV. Pods use PVC as volumes. StorageClass enables dynamic provisioning (cloud volumes: EBS, EFS, GCEPersistentDisk, AzureDisk). accessModes: ReadWriteOnce, ReadOnlyMany, ReadWriteMany.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the role of etcd in Kubernetes?
interviewQuestions.answer: etcd is a consistent, distributed key-value store that stores all cluster state and configuration — the single source of truth. It stores: pod specs, service definitions, ConfigMaps, Secrets, RBAC policies, cluster state. The API server is the only component that talks to etcd. Backup etcd regularly — losing etcd means losing the cluster. etcd uses RAFT consensus for consistency across nodes.

interviewQuestions.question: How does the scheduler assign pods to nodes?
interviewQuestions.answer: The scheduler watches for unscheduled pods (pod.spec.nodeName is empty) and assigns them to optimal nodes through a two-phase process: Filtering (predicates): remove nodes that don't meet pod requirements (resource requests, nodeSelector, affinity/anti-affinity, taints/tolerations). Scoring (priorities): rank remaining nodes by various criteria (resource availability, spreading, affinity, etc.). The highest-scoring node wins.

interviewQuestions.question: What is a Helm chart and why use it?
interviewQuestions.answer: A Helm chart is a package of pre-configured Kubernetes resources. It provides: templating (reusable, parameterized YAML), dependency management (subcharts), versioning (install/upgrade/rollback), release management (deploy same chart multiple times). Charts are structured: Chart.yaml (metadata), values.yaml (default config), templates/ (Kubernetes YAML with Go templates). Use Helm to package, share, and deploy complex applications.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the GitOps workflow with ArgoCD.
interviewQuestions.answer: GitOps uses Git as the single source of truth for declarative infrastructure and applications. ArgoCD: pull-based GitOps tool that continuously monitors a Git repository. When the Git repo changes, ArgoCD syncs the cluster to match. Benefits: audit trail (all changes via Git PR), easy rollback (git revert), single source of truth, automated drift correction. ArgoCD features: Application CRD, sync waves, hooks, SSO, multiple clusters.

interviewQuestions.question: What are taints and tolerations in Kubernetes?
interviewQuestions.answer: Taints are applied to nodes to repel pods that don't tolerate the taint. Tolerations are applied to pods to allow scheduling on tainted nodes. Taint effect: NoSchedule (don't schedule new pods), PreferNoSchedule (try to avoid), NoExecute (evict existing pods without toleration). Use cases: dedicated nodes (GPU-only), node isolation, node maintenance. taint: key=value:Effect, toleration: operator: Equal/Exists.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you upgrade a Kubernetes cluster?
interviewQuestions.answer: Upgrade order: first upgrade the control plane (one minor version at a time), then upgrade worker nodes. Steps: drain nodes (evict pods gracefully), upgrade kubeadm/kubelet/kubectl on each node, uncordon the node. Version skew policy: kubelet can be up to 2 minor versions behind the control plane. Always backup etcd before upgrades. Use managed K8s (EKS, AKS, GKE) for automated upgrades.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between RBAC roles and cluster roles?
interviewQuestions.answer: Role: grants permissions within a specific namespace. ClusterRole: grants permissions cluster-wide (non-namespaced resources, namespaced resources across all namespaces). RoleBinding: binds a Role to subjects (users, groups, service accounts) within a namespace. ClusterRoleBinding: binds a ClusterRole cluster-wide. Pattern: use ClusterRole for read-only access to all namespaces, Role for namespace-specific permissions.

books.title: Kubernetes in Action
books.author: Marko Lukša
books.description: Comprehensive guide to Kubernetes covering architecture, workloads, networking, storage, security, and cluster operations with hands-on examples.

books.title: Kubernetes Patterns
books.author: Bilgin Ibryam & Roland Huß
books.description: Reusable patterns for designing cloud-native applications on Kubernetes covering foundational, behavioral, structural, and configuration patterns.

books.title: The Kubernetes Book
books.author: Nigel Poulton & Pushkaraj Kulkarni
books.description: Accessible introduction to Kubernetes covering pods, Deployments, Services, storage, security, and production operations with real-world examples.

books.title: Programming Kubernetes
books.author: Michael Hausenblas & Stefan Schimanski
books.description: Extend Kubernetes programmatically: custom resources, controllers, operators, API extensions, and automation using client libraries.

books.title: Cloud Native DevOps with Kubernetes
books.author: John Arundel & Justin Domingus
books.description: Practical guide to deploying and operating cloud-native applications with Kubernetes covering CI/CD, monitoring, and production operations.

books.title: Kubernetes: Up and Running
books.author: Brendan Burns, Joe Beda, Kelsey Hightower
books.description: Dive into the future of infrastructure with this comprehensive guide by Kubernetes creators covering architecture, deployments, services, and cluster operations.

books.title: Managing Kubernetes
books.author: Brendan Burns & Craig Tracey
books.description: Operating Kubernetes clusters in production: cluster provisioning, monitoring, capacity planning, and day-2 operations.

books.title: GitOps and Kubernetes
books.author: Billy Yuen, Alexander Matyushentsev, Jesse Suen, Todd Ekenstam
books.description: GitOps principles, ArgoCD, and continuous delivery for Kubernetes applications with practical deployment patterns.

bestPractices: Set resource requests and limits on all containers — never rely on default unbounded resources
Use namespaces for environment isolation (dev, staging, prod) with ResourceQuotas
Use liveness, readiness, and startup probes for every pod for self-healing
Use PodDisruptionBudgets for critical workloads to maintain availability during node maintenance
Follow the principle of least privilege with RBAC — cluster-admin is for cluster-wide operators only
Use multiple replicas (>=2) for production workloads for high availability
Use rolling updates with maxSurge and maxUnavailable for zero-downtime deployments
Use infrastructure as code — store all manifests in Git, use GitOps (ArgoCD/Flux)
Use Helm or Kustomize for managing application configurations across environments
Use network policies to restrict pod-to-pod communication (zero-trust network model)
Use Secret resources (not ConfigMaps) for sensitive data — enable encryption at rest
Use external secrets operators (External Secrets, Sealed Secrets) for production secret management
Use pod anti-affinity and topology spread constraints for high availability across nodes/zones
Never run containers as root — set securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true
Use readiness probe for rolling updates — ensure new pods accept traffic before old ones terminate
Use PodSecurityAdmission to enforce security standards (restricted profile for user workloads)
Monitor everything — cluster metrics, pod metrics, logs, events, with proper alerting
Use PDBs for stateful workloads to prevent data loss during voluntary disruptions
Use init containers for setup tasks that must complete before app containers start
Regularly backup etcd — losing etcd means losing the entire cluster state

careerOpportunities.description: Manage and maintain Kubernetes clusters, upgrades, security, monitoring, and troubleshooting.
Build CI/CD pipelines, manage K8s infrastructure, implement GitOps, and automate operations.
Design and build cloud-native applications using Kubernetes-native patterns, microservices, and service meshes.
Build internal developer platforms on Kubernetes, providing self-service infrastructure to development teams.
Ensure reliability, performance, and scalability of Kubernetes-managed applications with SLOs and error budgets.
Design cloud-native architectures, advise on Kubernetes adoption, and guide technical strategy.
